policy_statement: Appoint a Chief State Resilience Officer (CSRO) to convene an adaptation and resilience sub-cabinet, oversee all State adaptation/resilience activities including bond expenditures, and ensure just transition principles are incorporated.
mechanism_description: The Governor should appoint a Chief State Resilience Officer to convene an adaptation/resilience sub-cabinet and oversee statewide resilience activities and bond spending, strengthening coordination and institutional capacity for adaptation while incorporating just transition principles.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance/institutions → ↑ adaptive capacity & coordinated resilience action → ↓ climate hazard exposure/vulnerability → ↓ climate impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human system: Establish or empower a central resilience governance role (e.g., Chief State Resilience Officer) and interagency coordination body to align agencies, prioritize projects, and manage resilience-related spending and standards.  
(2) Mediating process: Improved institutional capacity—shared risk information, integrated planning, clearer authority, faster decision-making, and better targeting/oversight of investments—raises the likelihood, speed, and effectiveness of adaptation implementation across sectors.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced damages and disruptions from climate hazards (e.g., flooding, heat, storms) due to better-prepared systems and more effectively delivered resilience investments; equity/just-transition principles can improve distribution of protective benefits but do not change the primary adaptation pathway.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved implementation of adaptation measures that reduce exposure and vulnerability to climate hazards through stronger institutional coordination and capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance coordination increases adaptive capacity, which enables adaptation actions that reduce exposure/vulnerability to climate hazards.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While improved coordination could incidentally support mitigation (e.g., aligning agencies on emissions policies) or resource efficiency (better procurement), the mechanism described is explicitly resilience/adaptation governance and bond spending oversight aimed at reducing climate impacts.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a similar coordination office were explicitly tasked primarily with emissions reduction delivery, it could shift to Mitigation; however, this canonical mechanism is resilience-governance-to-adaptive-capacity, so Adaptation remains primary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy instantiating this mechanism—resilience officer/sub-cabinet/coordination to build institutional adaptive capacity—primarily functions to improve adaptation readiness and implementation, not to directly change emissions or sinks.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any additional tagging (e.g., equity/just transition co-benefits, specific hazards addressed, or finance-governance subtype) requires policy-level evidence and is not set at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified if the appointed role’s dominant mandate is emissions-reduction coordination rather than resilience/adaptation; also, some institutional capacity measures yield indirect mitigation co-benefits, but these are secondary to the adaptation risk-reduction pathway here.
key_indicators: “Governor should appoint a Chief State Resilience Officer (CSRO)”; “convene an adaptation and resilience sub-cabinet”; “portfolio must include all State adaptation and resilience activities”; “oversight of relevant bond fund expenditures”; “ensure incorporation of just transition principles”; “executive-level coordination”
co_benefits: Improved disaster preparedness/response coordination; equity and workforce transition considerations (“just transition”); better fiscal oversight/accountability for public spending.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a regulatory-mandate (executive mandate establishing a role/reporting line), but the dominant function is cross-agency coordination and oversight (governance/planning) rather than prescribing performance requirements.
